title: Top-dressing perennial beds with compost
subtitle: When and how to apply compost to established beds, borders, and lawns
excerpt: Perennial beds and borders benefit from a top-dressing of compost once or twice per year. The compost feeds the soil biology, suppresses weeds, and improves water retention. For established beds, 3 to 5 cm of compost in late winter or early autumn is enough. The technique is different from mulching new beds, and the timing matters for not smothering crowns.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on mulching and soil improvement (2024). Charles Dowding perennial bed management notes. Garden Organic soil health and compost application guidance. Soil Association guidance on compost in organic growing (2022).

content.text: Perennial beds need compost applied differently from annual vegetable beds. Perennial plants have existing root systems and crowns that must not be buried. The goal is to feed the soil surface and let the organisms pull the compost down, not to work it into the ground.
Late winter (January to March): before most perennials are in active growth. Crowns are still compact and easy to work around. The compost warms the soil and feeds early root activity.
Autumn (October to November): after cutting back. Apply compost to the cleared bed. This feeds the soil over winter and improves structure before spring growth.
Skip the growing season (April to September): compost pressed against stems can rot susceptible crowns.

Remove any visible weeds from the bed surface before applying compost. Compost over the top of weeds mulches them effectively, but established perennial weeds (couch grass, bindweed) will push through.
Spread 3 to 5 cm of sieved or rough compost across the bed surface, working around (not over) the crowns and emerging shoots.
Pull compost back from individual plant stems and crowns by 5 cm. A build-up of moist compost against a stem can cause rot in wet winters.
Leave the surface undisturbed. No raking in, no digging. The worms will do this over the following weeks.

A lawn top-dressing with fine compost in autumn improves soil structure over several seasons. Apply 1 cm of fine sieved compost after scarifying or aerating the lawn. Work it into the surface with a stiff-bristle broom, moving in all directions until the grass blades are visible. Overseeding bare patches at the same time gives the best results. Avoid applying compost when the ground is waterlogged or in drought conditions.

Mature compost (one year or more old) is better than fresh compost for perennial beds. Fresh compost can burn new growth and introduce weed seeds if the heat cycle was incomplete. For a lawn top-dressing, sieve to 5 mm for a fine, even texture.
